spec:
Evaluate the focus dominance of the cat in the photograph. Score whether the cat is the sharpest and most crisply rendered element in the image relative to its surroundings. A cat rendered in clear focus while the background falls away into softness draws the viewer's eye naturally and effortlessly. A cat that is blurry, soft, or no sharper than everything else in the frame loses its focal authority.

This evaluation is not concerned with absolute image sharpness or technical resolution. It measures whether the cat holds a relative focus advantage over the rest of the scene — whether the optics of the photograph direct the viewer's attention toward the cat as the thing the camera was trying to see. A slightly soft image where the cat is still the sharpest element should score higher than a uniformly sharp image where the cat enjoys no focal advantage.

Score high when the cat is distinctly in focus while its surroundings are softer, creating a natural focal pull toward the cat. Score low when the cat is out of focus, equally sharp as everything else, or softer than the background — causing the viewer's eye to drift away from the cat rather than toward it.

Evaluate the subject prominence of the cat in the photograph. Score whether the cat occupies enough of the frame, with sufficient size and intentional placement, to register immediately as the subject of the image. A cat that fills a commanding portion of the frame and sits in a natural compositional position reads as the reason the photograph was taken. A cat that is tiny, pushed to the margins, awkwardly clipped at the edges, or dwarfed by the scale of its environment reads as incidental — something the photographer happened to include rather than intended to capture.

This evaluation measures whether the cat has been given enough visual real estate and positional weight to be perceived not merely as a thing in the image, but as the thing in the image. Consider the cat's size relative to the overall frame, its placement within the composition, and whether its spatial relationship to the borders of the image communicates intentionality.

Score high when the cat is large in the frame, well-positioned, and commands attention through sheer presence and scale. Score low when the cat is small, marginalized, lost in a vast expanse of background, or positioned in a way that makes it feel like an afterthought rather than the subject.

Evaluate the visual separation of the cat from its surroundings in the photograph. Score whether the cat is visually distinct from the background and free from competing elements that pull the viewer's attention away. A cat that blends into similarly colored or patterned surfaces — a gray cat on a gray couch, a tabby on a mottled rug, a black cat in a dark room — loses its identity as a distinct subject and becomes part of the scene's texture. Likewise, a photograph crowded with other animals, people, bright objects, or bold patterns forces the cat to compete for the viewer's gaze.

This evaluation measures whether the cat exists on its own visual plane — standing apart from both the background and any other elements in the scene through sufficient contrast in color, tone, texture, or spatial boundaries. It accounts for both camouflage against the background and clutter that creates visual competition.

Score high when the cat is clearly distinct from its surroundings, with strong tonal or color contrast against the background and no competing elements that rival the cat for the viewer's eye. Score low when the cat blends into the background, is camouflaged by similar colors or patterns, or is visually overshadowed by distracting elements elsewhere in the frame.
